content.problemStatement:
The state space of the output of this problem (and as a matter of fact, all the problems in this Cook-Off) is - 2 to the power T -
where T is the number of test cases (so be extra careful!). Each test case consists of T lines consisting of "YES" or "NO".
If a test case accurately represents the output that you would print for this file,
then print "YES" for this case. Print "NO" otherwise.



The output for a file is defined as the output for all the test cases one by one. If you output "YES" for test case 'x', then your output
must match the input for the test case 'x', and if and only if your output does not match the input for test case 'x', should you print "NO"
for that case.
constraints: Each line after the first line is exactly the string "YES" or "NO" (uppercase).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 100), the number of test cases. Then for each test case i (0-indexed) there are exactly T lines, each line being either "YES" or "NO". Thus the total number of lines after the first line is T·T.
content.outputFormat: For each test case i, output a single line containing "YES" if the i‑th line among the T lines of that test case is "YES" and all other T‑1 lines are "NO". Otherwise output "NO". The outputs are printed in order, one per line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: T=2. Test case 0 receives lines [NO, NO]; expected pattern is YES at position 0, NO at position 1 → mismatch → output NO. Test case 1 receives lines [NO, YES]; pattern matches (YES only at position 1) → output YES.
T=1. The only line is YES, which is exactly the required YES at position 0, so the answer is YES.
T=3. Each test case contains a single YES at its own index (0,1,2) and NO elsewhere, therefore all three test cases satisfy the condition.

solution.approach: Read T. For each test case index i from 0 to T‑1, read the next T strings. While reading, check the condition (j == i) == (string == "YES"). If any position violates the condition, the test case does not match and we will output "NO"; otherwise we output "YES". This is O(T²) time and O(1) extra space, well within limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T²) – we examine each of the T·T input strings exactly once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored besides the input buffer.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    T = int(data[0])
    idx = 1
    out_lines = []
    for i in range(T):
        match = True
        for j in range(T):
            s = data[idx]
            idx += 1
            is_yes = (s == 'YES')
            if (j == i) != is_yes:
                match = False
                # skip remaining strings of this test case
                idx += T - j - 1
                break
        out_lines.append('YES' if match else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
